facts.text: College hockey in the United States is played mainly through NCAA men's and women's programs, especially in northern states.
The Frozen Four is the final stage of the NCAA men's ice hockey tournament.
The Hobey Baker Award honors the top player in NCAA men's hockey each season.
Michigan, Minnesota, North Dakota, Denver, and Boston College are among the sport's most successful programs.
The Beanpot is a famous annual Boston tournament featuring Boston College, Boston University, Harvard, and Northeastern.
College hockey uses Olympic-sized and NHL-sized rinks depending on the venue, which can subtly affect style of play.
Many NHL players, especially American stars, developed through college hockey before turning pro.
Goalies often become local legends in college hockey because tournament games can hinge on hot goaltending.
The sport combines strong school rivalry culture with a pipeline to professional and Olympic competition.
Minnesota, Michigan, Massachusetts, and New York are major recruiting regions for college hockey talent.
Conference realignment has reshaped college hockey repeatedly, including the creation of the Big Ten hockey conference.
The NCAA tournament is single elimination, which gives college hockey a strong upset potential every spring.
Some of the sport's best atmospheres come from student sections that sing, chant, and taunt all game long.
Outdoor games at football stadiums became a popular event for some college hockey programs in the 2000s and 2010s.
Because teams play fewer games than in pro hockey, each college matchup can have outsized importance in rankings and postseason seeding.
Its championship weekend is the sport's biggest annual college showcase.
